sections.heading: Universal service broadband obligations
sections.title: Universal service broadband speed requirements and reporting
sections.summary: This section amends universal service obligations to require minimum broadband download speeds of at least 10 megabits per second. It allows the universal service order to specify additional characteristics of broadband services beyond minimum speed. The order must also provide guidance on matters related to service characteristics. OFCOM must consider this guidance when making regulations under universal service provider designation.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets minimum broadband speed requirements for universal service orders and requires OFCOM to review service specifications when speeds fall below 30 Mbps, impacting cross-border data transfer considerations for broadband infrastructure.

requirements.text: Universal service order must specify minimum download speed of at least 10 megabits per second
Universal service order may specify additional broadband service characteristics
Universal service order may provide guidance on matters relating to service characteristics
OFCOM must consider guidance in universal service order when making regulations

sections.source_quote: The universal service order may in particular say that broadband connections and services must be provided to any extent, but may not do so unless— (a) it specifies the minimum download speed that must be provided by those connections and services, and (b) the speed so specified is at least 10 megabits per second.

sections.heading: 72B Broadband download speeds: duty to give direction under
sections.title: Broadband speed compliance and review requirements
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to direct OFCOM to review the universal service order if it specifies a minimum download speed below 30 Mbps and sufficient broadband subscriptions exist at that speed. The review must assess whether higher minimum speeds are appropriate, and may include other matters related to universal service orders.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates review of universal service order minimum speeds when below 30 Mbps, affecting data transfer standards for broadband infrastructure across borders.

requirements.text: Secretary of State must direct OFCOM to review universal service order if minimum download speed specified is less than 30 Mbps
Review must assess appropriateness of higher minimum download speeds
Review may address other matters related to universal service orders

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must give OFCOM a direction under section 72A if— (a) the universal service order specifies a minimum download speed for broadband connections and services and the speed so specified is less than 30 megabits per second, and (b) it appears to the Secretary of State, on the basis of information published by OFCOM, that broadband connections or services that provide a minimum download speed of at least 30 megabits per second are subscribed to for use in at least 75% of premises in the United Kingdom.

sections.heading: Automatic compensation for failure to meet performance standards
sections.title: Broadband speed compliance and review requirements
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to direct OFCOM to review the universal service order if it specifies a minimum download speed below 30 Mbps and sufficient broadband subscriptions exist at that speed. The review must assess whether higher minimum speeds are appropriate, and may include other matters related to universal service orders.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates review of universal service order minimum speeds when below 30 Mbps, affecting data transfer standards for broadband infrastructure across borders.

requirements.text: Secretary of State must direct OFCOM to review universal service order if minimum download speed specified is less than 30 Mbps
Review must assess appropriateness of higher minimum download speeds
Review may address other matters related to universal service orders

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must give OFCOM a direction under section 72A if— (a) the universal service order specifies a minimum download speed for broadband connections and services and the speed so specified is less than 30 megabits per second, and (b) it appears to the Secretary of State, on the basis of information published by OFCOM, that broadband connections or services that provide a minimum download speed of at least 30 megabits per second are subscribed to for use in at least 75% of premises in the United Kingdom.

sections.heading: The electronic communications code
sections.title: Electronic communications code implementation
sections.summary: This section replaces the telecommunications code with a new electronic communications code. It modifies the Communications Act 2003 to apply the new code, changes terminology from 'conduits' to 'infrastructure', and updates references to specific sections of the Telecommunications Act 1984.
sections.dtia_summary: Replaces telecommunications code with electronic communications code, affecting data infrastructure standards and cross-border data transfer mechanisms for communications services.

requirements.text: Omit Schedule 2 (the telecommunications code) from Telecommunications Act 1984
Insert Schedule 3A into Communications Act 2003 before Schedule 4
Replace reference to 'conduits' with 'infrastructure' in relevant sections
Replace reference to 'conduit system' with 'system of infrastructure' in relevant sections

sections.source_quote: In the Telecommunications Act 1984 omit Schedule 2 (the telecommunications code). Before Schedule 4 to the Communications Act 2003 insert Schedule 3A set out in Schedule 1 to this Act.

sections.heading: Power to make transitional provision in connection with the code
sections.title: Transitional provisions for electronic communications code
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to make transitional regulations for the electronic communications code. Regulations may amend Schedule 2 and require parliamentary approval if they include provisions related to primary legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for smooth implementation of electronic communications code, affecting data transfer standards and infrastructure compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may make transitional regulations for electronic communications code
Regulations may amend Schedule 2
Regulations requiring parliamentary approval must have draft laid before each House of Parliament

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations made by statutory instrument make transitional, transitory or saving provision in connection with the coming into force of section 4 and Schedule 1.

sections.heading: Power to make consequential provision etc in connection with the code
sections.title: Consequential provisions for electronic communications code
sections.summary: This section enables the Secretary of State to make consequential regulations for the electronic communications code. Regulations may amend, repeal, or modify other legislation and require parliamentary approval if they affect primary legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for adjustments to existing laws when implementing electronic communications code, impacting data transfer and infrastructure compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may make consequential regulations for electronic communications code
Regulations may amend, repeal, or modify application of other legislation
Regulations requiring parliamentary approval must have draft laid before each House of Parliament

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations make consequential provision in connection with any provision made by or under section 4 or this section or Schedule 1.

sections.heading: Application of the code: protection of the environment
sections.title: Environmental compliance for electronic communications regulations
sections.summary: This section modifies subsection (2B) of section 109(2A) in the Communications Act 2003 to require that regulations restricting electronic communications must comply with environmental protection duties under National Parks legislation if they expire before April 6, 2018. The Secretary of State must comply with subsection (2)(b) for regulations to be deemed environmentally compliant.

requirements.text: Subsection (2B) applies if the Secretary of State has complied with subsection (2)(b) in connection with any particular exercise of the power to make regulations under this section

sections.source_quote: For section 109(2A) of the Communications Act 2003 (under which regulations that set restrictions and conditions to the application of the electronic communications code are deemed by subsection (2B) to comply with duties under National Parks and other legislation if they comply with the duty to have regard to the need to protect the environment, but only if they expire before 6 April 2018) substitute—"(2A) Subsection (2B) applies if the Secretary of State has complied with subsection (2)(b) in connection with any particular exercise of the power to make regulations under this section."

sections.heading: Regulation of dynamic spectrum access services
sections.title: Dynamic spectrum access service registration and enforcement
sections.summary: This section establishes registration requirements for dynamic spectrum access service providers with OFCOM, including application procedures, publication of criteria, registration duration, and revocation/variation processes. It mandates detailed notifications for proposed revocations, requires a register of providers, and specifies fee structures for registration and related activities. The regulations also outline enforcement mechanisms including notifications for contraventions, penalties, and confirmation decisions.

requirements.text: A person who provides, or proposes to provide, a dynamic spectrum access service may be registered under this section by OFCOM
An application for registration under this section must be made to OFCOM and contain such information as OFCOM may reasonably require
OFCOM must, from time to time as they think fit, publish the criteria for determining applications and information relating to restrictions and conditions for registration
The criteria published under subsection (3)(a) may include different provision for different cases
Registration under this section is to be for such period, and subject to such restrictions and conditions, as OFCOM think fit
Any such restrictions and conditions are to be contained in a notice in writing given to the person registered under this section
OFCOM may revoke a registration under section 53A, or vary the restrictions and conditions to which it is subject by notice in writing given to the person registered under that section
OFCOM may revoke a registration under section 53A, or vary the restrictions and conditions to which it is subject by a general notice applicable to the class to which the person belongs
Where OFCOM propose to revoke or vary a registration, they must give the person registered under section 53A a notification stating the reason for the proposed revocation or variation

Where OFCOM propose to revoke or vary a registration, they must give the person registered under section 53A a notification specifying the period during which the person notified has an opportunity to make representations about the proposal
OFCOM must establish and maintain a register for the purposes of this Part
The register is to contain the names of the persons registered under section 53A
The register is to contain such other information relating to the registration of those persons as OFCOM consider appropriate
OFCOM may make available to users or prospective users of dynamic spectrum access services such information contained in the register as they consider appropriate
Regulations made by OFCOM may provide for OFCOM to charge fees for registering a person under section 53A
Regulations made by OFCOM may provide for OFCOM to charge fees for the continuation in force of such a registration
Regulations made by OFCOM may provide for OFCOM to charge fees for the variation or revocation of such a registration
Regulations made by OFCOM may provide for OFCOM to charge fees for anything done by OFCOM in connection with facilitating the service provided by a person registered under section 53A
OFCOM may require such security to be given, by way of deposit or otherwise, for the payment of those sums as they think fit
A sum which is required to be paid to OFCOM by virtue of the regulations must be paid to OFCOM as soon as it becomes payable in accordance with the regulations

OFCOM may give a notification to a person registered under section 53A if there are reasonable grounds for believing that the person is contravening, or has contravened, the restrictions or conditions subject to which the person is registered
A notification must set out the determination made by OFCOM
A notification must specify the restriction or condition and contravention in respect of which that determination has been made
A notification must specify the period during which the person notified has an opportunity to make representations
A notification must specify the steps that OFCOM think should be taken by the person to comply with the restriction or condition
A notification must specify the steps that OFCOM think should be taken by the person to remedy the consequences of the contravention
A notification may contain a statement that OFCOM are minded to suspend or revoke the person's registration
A notification must specify any penalty which OFCOM are minded to impose in accordance with section 53F

Where a notification has been given to a person in respect of a contravention of a restriction or condition, OFCOM may give a further notification in respect of the same contravention if the contravention is one occurring after the time of the giving of the earlier notification

Where a notification has been given to a person in respect of a contravention of a restriction or condition, OFCOM may give a further notification in respect of the same contravention if the contravention is a continuing contravention and the subsequent notification is in respect of so much of a period as falls after a period to which the earlier notification relates

Where a notification has been given to a person in respect of a contravention of a restriction or condition, OFCOM may give a further notification in respect of the same contravention if the earlier notification has been withdrawn without a penalty having been imposed in respect of the notified contravention
Where a notification relates to more than one contravention, a separate penalty may be specified in respect of each contravention
Where a notification relates to a continuing contravention, no more than one penalty may be specified in respect of the period of contravention specified in the notification

The amount of a penalty specified under subsection (4) is to be such amount, not exceeding £20,000 per day, as OFCOM determine to be proportionate to the contravention in respect of which it is imposed

The amount of any other penalty specified under this section is to be such amount, not exceeding £2 million, as OFCOM determine to be proportionate to the contravention in respect of which it is imposed

OFCOM may require a person to whom a notification has been given under section 53E to provide them with all such information as they may require for the purpose of ascertaining the person's gross revenue
A demand for information required under this section must be contained in a notice given to the person from whom the information is required
A person required to give information under this section must provide it in such manner and within such reasonable period as may be specified by OFCOM

OFCOM may require a person falling within subsection (2) to provide a person registered under section 53A with all such information as OFCOM consider necessary and proportionate for the purpose of enabling the registered person to avoid undue interference with wireless telegraphy

The persons falling within this subsection are a person who is using, or has established or used, a wireless telegraphy station, and a person who is using, or has installed or used, wireless telegraphy apparatus
A demand for information required under this section must be contained in a notice given to the person from whom the information is required
The notice must describe the required information
The notice must specify the manner and form in which it is to be provided
The notice must specify when and (if appropriate) how frequently it is to be provided
The notice must specify to whom it is to be provided

sections.source_quote: OFCOM may require a person to whom a notification has been given under section 53E to provide them with all such information as they may require for the purpose of ascertaining the person's gross revenue.

sections.title: OFCOM fee payment for wireless telegraphy licenses
sections.summary: This section amends the Wireless Telegraphy Act 2006 to specify that fees paid to OFCOM for wireless telegraphy licenses must include amounts charged under section 53D of the Digital Economy Act 2017. It also modifies penalty calculations to exclude certain provisions and adjusts the scope of relevant contraventions for penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines fee structures for wireless telegraphy licenses but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to spectrum licensing and not data flows.

requirements.text: Fees paid to OFCOM must include amounts charged under section 53D of the Digital Economy Act 2017

sections.source_quote: an amount paid to OFCOM in respect of a fee charged under section 53D of that Act

sections.heading: Parliamentary procedure for designation of age-verification regulator
sections.title: Parliamentary procedure for age-verification regulator designation
sections.summary: This section outlines the parliamentary procedure for designating an age-verification regulator, which is prospective and relates to online pornography regulations. It specifies that the regulator has the power to require information, enforce sections 14 and 18, and impose financial penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a regulatory framework for age-verification systems but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to online content regulation and not data flows.

requirements.text: Parliamentary procedure for designation of age-verification regulator

sections.source_quote: Parliamentary procedure for designation of age-verification regulator

sections.heading: Meaning of "extreme pornographic material"
sections.title: Definition of 'extreme pornographic material'
sections.summary: This section defines 'extreme pornographic material' as content that is prohibited under the Online Safety Act 2023. It specifies that the regulator has the power to require internet service providers to block access to such material but cannot issue notices if the action would be detrimental to national security.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines prohibited content categories but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to online content regulation and not data flows.

requirements.text: Regulator may require internet service providers to block access to extreme pornographic material
Regulator cannot give notices under section 23(1) if actions would be detrimental to national security

sections.source_quote: Meaning of 'extreme pornographic material'

sections.heading: Lending of e-books by public libraries
sections.title: E-book lending by public libraries
sections.summary: This section amends the Public Lending Right Act 1979 to define 'lent out' as making available e-books to the public for limited time away from library premises via electronic transmission. It also modifies the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 to require that e-book lending complies with purchase or licensing terms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines e-book lending practices but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to library services and not data flows.

requirements.text: E-books must be made available to the public for limited time away from library premises via electronic transmission
E-book lending must comply with purchase or licensing terms

sections.source_quote: lent out means made available to a member of the public for use away from library premises for a limited time (including by being communicated by means of electronic transmission to a place other than library premises)

sections.heading: Offences: infringing copyright and making available right
sections.title: Copyright infringement offenses
sections.summary: This section amends the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 to expand the definition of copyright infringement offenses. It requires that infringers must know or have reason to believe they are infringing copyright, and must intend to make a gain or know that their actions will cause loss to copyright owners.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines copyright infringement offenses but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to intellectual property rights and not data flows.

requirements.text: Infringers must know or have reason to believe they are infringing copyright
Infringers must intend to make a gain or know that their actions will cause loss to copyright owners

sections.source_quote: A person who infringes copyright in a work by communicating the work to the public commits an offence if P—(a) knows or has reason to believe that P is infringing copyright in the work, and (b) either—(i) intends to make a gain for P or another person, or (ii) knows or has reason to believe that communicating the work to the public will cause loss to the owner of the copyright, or will expose the owner of the copyright to a risk of loss.

sections.heading: Registered designs: infringement: marking product with internet link
sections.title: Registered designs: internet link marking
sections.summary: This section amends the Registered Designs Act 1949 to allow for the marking of products with internet links that reference registered designs. It specifies that such links must be publicly accessible and clearly associate the product with the design number.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines requirements for product marking with internet links but has no direct relevance to data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to intellectual property rights and not data flows.

requirements.text: Internet links must be publicly accessible and clearly associate the product with the design number

sections.source_quote: The reference in subsection (2) to a relevant internet link is a reference to an address of a posting on the internet—(a) which is accessible to the public free of charge, and (b) which clearly associates the product with the number of the design.

sections.heading: Copyright etc where broadcast retransmitted by cable
sections.title: Repeal of copyright and broadcasting exceptions
sections.summary: This section repeals specific provisions from the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 and the Broadcasting Act 1996 that previously allowed for copyright exceptions when broadcast signals were retransmitted via cable. It also repeals related regulations from the Copyright and Related Rights Regulations 2003. The repeal applies to certain sections and schedules that previously addressed copyright and performance rights in cable retransmission scenarios.

requirements.text: Repeal sections 73 and 73A of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
Repeal paragraphs 19 and 19A of Schedule 2 of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
Repeal section 134(3A) and the phrase 'Subject to subsection (3A)' in section 134(1) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
Repeal section 149(za) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
Repeal section 205B(1)(cc) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
Repeal section 138 of the Broadcasting Act 1996 and Schedule 9 of the Broadcasting Act 1996
Repeal regulation 22 of the Copyright and Related Rights Regulations 2003 (S.I. 2003/2498)

sections.source_quote: In the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 the following are repealed— (a) sections 73 and 73A (copyright not infringed where broadcast retransmitted by cable); (b) paragraphs 19 and 19A of Schedule 2 (rights in relation to performance or recording not infringed where broadcast retransmitted by cable).

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to improve public service delivery
sections.title: Disclosure of information for public service improvement
sections.summary: This section allows specified persons to disclose information held for their functions to other specified persons for objectives related to improving public services or facilitating benefits. The disclosure must align with specified objectives that target public service improvement or benefit provision, including health, well-being, and charitable purposes. The appropriate national authority can amend Schedule 4 to include new persons or objectives, but disclosures must comply with conditions related to public service improvement and well-being.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for sharing information between public entities to improve services, requiring that disclosures target public service improvement or benefit provision for individuals, households, or undertakings.

requirements.text: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for the purposes of a specified objective
The specified objective must have the purpose of improving or targeting a public service provided to individuals, households, or undertakings
The specified objective must have the purpose of facilitating the provision of a benefit (whether or not financial) to individuals, households, or undertakings
The specified objective must have the purpose of supporting the delivery of a specified person's functions or the administration, monitoring, or enforcement of those functions
The specified person must be a person specified or of a description specified in Schedule 4
The appropriate national authority may amend Schedule 4 to add, remove, or modify entries relating to persons or descriptions of persons
The appropriate national authority may amend the scope of disclosures to include new persons or descriptions of persons

sections.source_quote: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for the purposes of an objective which is a specified objective in relation to each of those persons.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to gas and electricity suppliers etc
sections.title: Disclosure of information to gas and electricity suppliers for fuel poverty relief
sections.summary: This section permits specified persons to disclose information to licensed gas or electricity suppliers for the purpose of assisting people living in fuel poverty. The disclosure must be for reducing energy costs, improving energy efficiency, or other fuel poverty measures. The recipient must use the information for specific purposes under the Energy Act 2010, Gas Act 1986, or Electricity Act 1989, including support schemes for fuel poverty. The appropriate national authority can amend Schedule 5 to include new persons or disclosures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables sharing of information with energy suppliers to address fuel poverty, requiring disclosures to align with specific fuel poverty measures and energy-related regulations.

requirements.text: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to a licensed gas supplier or licensed electricity supplier
The disclosure must be for the purpose of assisting people living in fuel poverty by reducing energy costs
The disclosure must be for the purpose of improving efficiency in the use of energy
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the recipient for support schemes under the Energy Act 2010
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the recipient for obligations imposed by orders under the Gas Act 1986
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the recipient for obligations imposed by orders under the Electricity Act 1989
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the recipient for making grants under the Social Security Act 1990
The appropriate national authority may amend Schedule 5 to add, remove, or modify entries relating to persons or descriptions of persons
The appropriate national authority may amend the scope of disclosures to include new persons or descriptions of persons

sections.source_quote: If the first and second conditions are met, a specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to a licensed gas supplier or licensed electricity supplier.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information by gas and electricity suppliers etc
sections.title: Disclosure of information by gas and electricity suppliers for fuel poverty relief
sections.summary: This section allows persons who receive information under section 36 to disclose it to specified persons for the purpose of assisting people living in fuel poverty. The disclosure must align with specific objectives related to reducing energy costs, improving energy efficiency, or other fuel poverty measures in England and Wales or Scotland.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables energy suppliers to share information with public entities to address fuel poverty, requiring disclosures to target specific fuel poverty measures.

requirements.text: A person to whom information may be disclosed under section 36 may disclose information held by that person to a specified person
The disclosure must be for the purpose of assisting people living in fuel poverty in England and Wales or Scotland
The disclosure must be for the purpose of reducing energy costs
The disclosure must be for the purpose of improving efficiency in the use of energy

sections.source_quote: If the condition in subsection (2) is met, a person to whom information may be disclosed under section 36 may disclose information held by that person to a specified person.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to water and sewerage undertakers etc
sections.title: Disclosure of information to water and sewerage undertakers for water poverty relief
sections.summary: This section permits specified persons to disclose information to water or sewerage undertakers for the purpose of assisting people living in water poverty. The disclosure must align with specific objectives related to reducing water or sewerage costs, improving water efficiency, or water poverty measures. The recipient must use the information for compliance with water poverty schemes under the Water Industry Act 1991 or Flood and Water Management Act 2010.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables sharing of information with water and sewerage providers to address water poverty, requiring disclosures to align with specific water poverty measures.

requirements.text: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to a water or sewerage undertaker for an area which is wholly or mainly in England or Wales
The disclosure must be for the purpose of assisting eligible people by reducing water or sewerage costs
The disclosure must be for the purpose of improving efficiency in the use of water
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the undertaker for compliance with water poverty schemes under the Water Industry Act 1991
The information must be disclosed with the intention that it will be used by the undertaker for social tariffs under the Flood and Water Management Act 2010

sections.source_quote: If the first and second conditions are met, a specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to a water or sewerage undertaker for an area which is wholly or mainly in England, or a water or sewerage undertaker for an area which is wholly or mainly in Wales.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information by water and sewerage undertakers etc
sections.title: Disclosure of information by water and sewerage undertakers for water poverty relief
sections.summary: This section allows persons who receive information under section 38 to disclose it to specified persons for the purpose of assisting people living in water poverty. The disclosure must align with specific objectives related to reducing water or sewerage costs, improving water efficiency, or water poverty measures in England and Wales.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables water and sewerage providers to share information with public entities to address water poverty, requiring disclosures to target specific water poverty measures.

requirements.text: A person to whom information may be disclosed under section 38 may disclose information held by that person to a specified person
The disclosure must be for the purpose of assisting eligible people in England and Wales by reducing water or sewerage costs
The disclosure must be for the purpose of improving efficiency in the use of water

sections.source_quote: If the condition in subsection (2) is met, a person to whom information may be disclosed under section 38 may disclose information held by that person to a specified person.

sections.heading: Further provisions about disclosures under any of sections 35 to 39
sections.title: Restrictions on use of disclosed personal information
sections.summary: Personal information disclosed under sections 35-39 may only be used by the recipient for the original disclosure purposes, except where the recipient is the Revenue and Customs and consents to broader use. This restriction applies to all disclosures made under those sections, with specific exceptions for certain authorities and consent mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts how disclosed data can be used across borders or with third parties; requires purpose limitation for data sharing activities to prevent misuse.
sections.authority: Commissioners for Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs

requirements.text: Personal information disclosed under sections 35 to 39 may only be used by the recipient for the purposes for which it was disclosed
The recipient may use the information for legal proceedings (civil or criminal)
The recipient may use the information for the purposes of a specified person or a person to whom information may be disclosed under section 36 or 38
The recipient may use the information for the purposes of behaviour
The recipient may use the information for the purposes of conviction

Information disclosed to Revenue and Customs under sections 35, 36 or 38 may be used for purposes other than those for which it was disclosed with consent of the Commissioners for Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs

sections.source_quote: Personal information disclosed under any of sections 35 to 39 may only be used by the person to whom it is disclosed for the purposes for which it was disclosed, subject to subsection (2).

sections.heading: Interpretation of this Chapter etc

sections.title: Consequential provisions for statutory instruments
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to make regulations via statutory instrument that amend, repeal, or revoke provisions of enactments passed before or during the same parliamentary session as the Digital Economy Act 2017. These regulations must be made by statutory instrument and may include transitional provisions. The regulations require parliamentary approval for amendments to Acts and are subject to annulment by either House of Parliament if not approved.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs how statutory instruments can modify or repeal existing laws, which is relevant for DTIAs when assessing the legal impact of cross-border data transfers through regulatory mechanisms.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may make regulations amending, repealing or revoking provisions of enactments passed before or in the same session as this Act
Regulations under this section must be made by statutory instrument
Regulations may make different provision for different purposes
Regulations may contain transitional or transitory provision or savings
Statutory instruments amending Acts must be approved by resolution of each House of Parliament
Statutory instruments not amending Acts are subject to annulment by either House of Parliament

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations make the provision in subsection (2) in consequence of any provision made by section 46.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to reduce debt owed to the public sector
sections.title: Disclosure of information to reduce public sector debt
sections.summary: This section permits specified persons to disclose personal information to other specified persons for debt collection purposes owed to public authorities or the Crown. Debt is defined as unpaid amounts after payment deadlines. Disclosure includes identifying, collecting, or taking legal action on debt. The disclosure must be for specific purposes and may be amended by regulations under the relevant national authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows cross-border data transfers for debt collection purposes, requiring specific safeguards to ensure information is used only for debt recovery and not for other purposes.

requirements.text: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for debt collection purposes
Debt is owed to a public authority or Crown if a payment is required and remains unpaid after the deadline
Taking action on debt includes identifying, collecting, bringing civil proceedings, or taking administrative action
The appropriate national authority may amend Schedule 7 to add persons or descriptions of persons
Regulations adding persons to Schedule 7 must satisfy conditions including the person's ability to identify, manage or recover debt

sections.source_quote: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for the purposes of the taking of action in connection with debt owed to a public authority or to the Crown.

sections.heading: Further provisions about power in section 48
sections.title: Use restrictions for disclosed debt information
sections.summary: This section restricts how personal information disclosed under section 48 may be used. It permits use only for the original purpose, legal proceedings, safeguarding vulnerable adults/children, or national security. The information must not be disclosed to third parties without consent or for other purposes. This section also defines when information identifies a particular person and specifies that disclosures do not breach data protection legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict use restrictions on cross-border data transfers for debt collection, ensuring information is only used for debt recovery and not for other purposes.

requirements.text: Personal information disclosed under section 48 may only be used for the purposes for which it was disclosed
Information may be used for legal proceedings, safeguarding vulnerable adults/children, or national security
Information may not be disclosed to third parties without consent
Information may not be used for purposes other than those for which it was disclosed

sections.source_quote: Personal information disclosed under section 48 may only be used by the person to whom it is disclosed for the purposes for which it was disclosed, subject to [a] purpose, [b] behaviour, [c] for the purposes of legal proceedings (whether civil or criminal), [d] for the purposes of safeguarding vulnerable adults or children, or [e] for the purposes of protecting national security.

sections.heading: Duty to review operation of Chapter
"the appropriate national authority" means the relevant Minister, subject to
Disclosure of information to combat fraud against the public sector
sections.title: Disclosure of information for public sector fraud prevention
sections.summary: A specified person may disclose information held for public sector fraud prevention purposes to another specified person. The disclosure must be for taking action against fraud against a public authority, which includes preventing, detecting, investigating, prosecuting, civil proceedings, or administrative actions related to fraud. Fraud against a public authority is defined as loss to a public authority or exposure to risk of loss. A public authority is defined as entities exercising public functions, funded by public money, or appointed bodies. The disclosure must be limited to the purpose for which it was disclosed, except for legal proceedings or specific consent.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a mechanism for sharing information across entities to combat public sector fraud, requiring that disclosures be limited to the intended purpose and compliant with data protection laws to ensure data transfers are lawful and minimally restrictive.

requirements.text: A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for the purposes of taking action in connection with fraud against a public authority.
Fraud against a public authority means a fraud offence involving loss to a public authority or exposure to risk of loss.
Fraud offence means an offence under section 1 of the Fraud Act 2006 or, in relation to Scotland, an offence of fraud.
Taking action in connection with fraud against a public authority includes preventing, detecting, investigating, prosecuting, civil proceedings, or administrative actions related to fraud.
A specified person means a person specified or of a description specified in Schedule 8.
Personal information disclosed under section 56 may only be used by the person to whom it is disclosed for the purposes for which it was disclosed, subject to legal proceedings or consent.

sections.source_quote: Disclosure of information to combat fraud against the public sector (1) A specified person may disclose information held by the person in connection with any of the person's functions to another specified person for the purposes of the taking of action in connection with fraud against a public authority.

sections.heading: Further provisions about power in section 56
sections.title: Restrictions on use of disclosed information for fraud prevention
sections.summary: Personal information disclosed under section 56 may only be used for the intended purpose, legal proceedings, or with consent. The disclosure does not breach data protection legislation. Information that identifies a particular person must be handled with care to avoid misuse. The section does not apply to information disclosed by the Revenue and Customs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict use restrictions on information shared for fraud prevention, ensuring it is not misused beyond the intended purpose and aligns with data protection laws to prevent unauthorized data transfers.

sections.heading: Duty to review operation of Chapter
sections.title: Review of Chapter operation
sections.summary: The relevant Minister must review the operation of the Chapter after three years of its coming into force. The review must include published criteria and consultation with stakeholders. If amendments are needed, the Minister may do so with consent from relevant authorities in Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires periodic review of the fraud information sharing mechanism to ensure it remains effective and compliant with evolving data protection standards.

requirements.text: The relevant Minister must review the operation of the Chapter after three years of its coming into force.

sections.source_quote: As soon as is reasonably practicable after the end of three years beginning with the day on which this Chapter comes into force, the relevant Minister must review its operation for the purposes of deciding whether it should be amended or repealed.

sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'appropriate national authority', 'public authority', 'Northern Ireland body', 'Scottish body', and 'Welsh body'. It specifies that the appropriate national authority varies by jurisdiction: Scottish Ministers for Scotland, Welsh Ministers for Wales, and the Department of Finance in Northern Ireland for Northern Ireland. The definitions are contextual to the Digital Economy Act 2017 and related legislation.

Scottish body means a person who is a part of the Scottish Administration, a Scottish public authority with mixed functions or no reserved functions, or a person providing services to a person within paragraph (a) or (b)

sections.heading: Disclosure of information for research purposes
sections.title: Disclosure of personal information for research purposes
sections.summary: This section permits public authorities to disclose personal information for research purposes, but imposes strict conditions: the information must be anonymized (identity not specified and not deducible), the research must be accredited, all involved parties must take reasonable steps to prevent accidental or deliberate disclosure, and the disclosure must be for processing under the same conditions. The disclosure is limited to accredited entities and must comply with a code of practice.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for cross-border transfer of personal information for research, requiring anonymization, accreditation, and adherence to a code of practice to ensure data is not identifiable and is handled securely.

requirements.text: Information held by a public authority in connection with the authority's functions may be disclosed to another person for the purposes of research which is being or is to be carried out

Personal information may be disclosed for the purpose of processing it for disclosure under subsection (1) by a public authority to a person involved in processing the information for that purpose
Personal information may be disclosed for the purpose of processing it for disclosure under subsection (1) by one such person to another such person, subject to sections 67(5), 68(5) and 69(5)

sections.heading: Accreditation for the purposes of this Chapter
sections.title: Accreditation for statistical processing and disclosure
sections.summary: The Statistics Board may accredit individuals and research for specific information processing and disclosure activities under section 64. The Board must publish conditions for accreditation and grounds for withdrawal, including adherence to a code of practice. Accredited persons must be registered in public registers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes accreditation requirements for entities handling statistical data disclosures, including fit and proper checks and public interest criteria. It impacts DTIAs by requiring specific accreditation processes for data handlers and research entities.

sections.heading: Disclosure of non-identifying information by the Revenue and Customs
sections.title: Disclosure of non-identifying information by Revenue and Customs
sections.summary: Revenue and Customs officials may disclose non-identifying information to any person if it is non-identifying and the disclosure serves the public interest. Non-identifying information is defined as information that has never been identifying or created by combining identifying information without becoming identifying itself. Identifying information relates to a person's identity. The section specifies the meaning of key terms like 'Revenue and Customs official' and 'function of the Revenue and Customs'.

The information is non-identifying information
The official thinks that the disclosure would be in the public interest
Information is non-identifying information if it is not, and has never been, identifying information
Information is non-identifying information if it has been created by combining identifying information but is not itself identifying information
Information is identifying information if it relates to a person whose identity is known

sections.heading: Disclosure of employer reference information by the Revenue and Customs

sections.summary: Revenue and Customs officials may disclose employer reference information to the Employers' Liability Tracing Office for specific purposes related to personal injury claims or mesothelioma payments. The permitted purposes are claims arising from employment-related injuries or deaths, and applications for mesothelioma payments under the 2014 Act. Employer reference information includes employer names, addresses, and unique identifiers used by Revenue and Customs. The definition explicitly covers former employers and aligns with employment income tax definitions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific conditions for cross-border data disclosure to a third party (Employers' Liability Tracing Office) for limited purposes, requiring precise definition of employer reference data and explicit permitted uses. sections.title: Amendments to disclosure of HMRC information to Statistics Board
sections.summary: This section amends the Statistics and Registration Service Act 2007 to modify disclosure conditions for HMRC information to the Statistics Board. It requires Commissioners to consider the Board's views when determining disclosure conditions, prohibits using disclosed information for statistical services without consent, and mandates adherence to Data Protection Act 1998 codes of practice. The amendments ensure data protection compliance while allowing statistical use.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes specific conditions on cross-border data transfers to the Statistics Board, requiring consent for statistical use and adherence to data protection codes. Critical for DTIA when transferring personal data internationally to statistical bodies.

sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes strict conditions for cross-border data transfers from public authorities to statistical bodies, requiring consent for statistical use and adherence to data protection codes. Essential for DTIA when transferring public sector data internationally.

sections.heading: Provision of children's programmes
sections.title: OFCOM criteria for children's programmes
sections.summary: OFCOM may publish criteria for children's programmes, requiring licensed public service channels to meet these criteria. sections.heading: Suspension of radio licences for inciting crime or disorder
sections.title: Suspension of radio licences for inciting crime
sections.summary: OFCOM must suspend a radio licence if the holder includes programmes likely to incite crime or disorder, fails to comply with a licence condition, and the failure justifies revocation. A 21-day period allows for representations before potential revocation, with revocation taking effect after 28 days.
sections.dtia_summary: Suspension of radio licences for inciting crime impacts data transfer assessments by requiring content monitoring and reporting mechanisms for potentially harmful content.

sections.heading: "24A Duty to prevent access to seriously harmful extrinsic material
sections.title: Preventing access to harmful digital content
sections.summary: OFCOM must take all reasonable steps to prevent digital services from enabling public access to seriously harmful extrinsic material. This material is defined as content not included in the service that appears to OFCOM to have the potential to cause serious harm or incite crime. The definition includes content likely to encourage criminal activity or lead to disorder.

sections.heading: I119
sections.title: Direct marketing code requirements
sections.summary: The Commissioner must prepare a code of practice for direct marketing that includes practical guidance on compliance with data protection laws and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations. The code must be developed after consultation with trade associations, data subjects, and representatives of data subjects' interests. 'Good practice' requires compliance with the code and relevant regulations.

sections.summary: The Secretary of State may designate strategic priorities for telecommunications, radio spectrum management, and postal services. OFCOM must consider these priorities when performing their functions and provide a 40-day written explanation of their actions. OFCOM must also publish annual reviews of their implementation of the strategic priorities within 12 months of designation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes strategic priorities that impact regulatory oversight and information sharing between OFCOM and the government, relevant for DTIA in regulatory compliance contexts.

sections.heading: S C H E D U L E S
sections.title: Definitions of key concepts in electronic communications code
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Electronic Communications Code, including 'operator', 'code right', 'statutory purposes', and 'electronic communications apparatus'. It specifies that 'operator' refers to the person or Secretary of State/Northern Ireland department depending on context, while 'code right' encompasses rights to install, maintain, inspect, and operate communications infrastructure. The definitions also clarify what constitutes electronic communications apparatus and related structures.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for understanding data infrastructure rights but does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to physical telecommunications infrastructure rather than data.

requirements.text: In this code 'operator' means where this code is applied in any person's case by a direction under section 106, that person
In this code 'operator' means where this code applies by virtue of section 106(3)(b), the Secretary of State or (as the case may be) the Northern Ireland department
For the purposes of this code a 'code right' in relation to an operator and any land is a right for the statutory purposes
In this code 'electronic communications apparatus' means apparatus designed or adapted for use in connection with the provision of an electronic communications network

In this code 'electronic communications apparatus' means apparatus designed or adapted for a use which consists of or includes the sending or receiving of communications or other signals transmitted by means of an electronic communications network

In this code 'line' means any wire, cable, tube, pipe or similar thing (including its casing or coating) designed or adapted for use in connection with the provision of any electronic communications network or electronic communications service
In this code 'structure' includes a building only if the sole purpose of that building is to enclose other electronic communications apparatus

sections.heading: The operator's network
sections.title: Definition of operator's network and infrastructure system
sections.summary: This section defines 'network' and 'infrastructure system' for the Electronic Communications Code. 'Network' refers to the operator's electronic communications network or infrastructure system, while 'infrastructure system' is a system available for use by providers of electronic communications networks. The definitions specify that references to provision of an infrastructure system include establishing or maintaining such systems.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines infrastructure terms relevant to telecommunications but does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to physical network infrastructure rather than data.

requirements.text: In this code 'network' in relation to an operator means—(a) if the operator falls within paragraph 2(a), so much of any electronic communications network or infrastructure system provided by the operator as is not excluded from the application of the code under section 106(5), and (b) if the operator falls within paragraph 2(b), the electronic communications network which the Secretary of State or the Northern Ireland department is providing or proposing to provide

sections.source_quote: In this code 'network' in relation to an operator means—(a) if the operator falls within paragraph 2(a), so much of any electronic communications network or infrastructure system provided by the operator as is not excluded from the application of the code under section 106(5), and (b) if the operator falls within paragraph 2(b), the electronic communications network which the Secretary of State or the Northern Ireland department is providing or proposing to provide.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Operator's network upgrades and sharing with conditions
sections.summary: An operator may upgrade or share electronic communications apparatus with another operator if the changes cause no adverse impact on appearance and impose no additional burden on the other party. Additional burden includes adverse effects on land enjoyment or increased costs. Agreements void if they prevent upgrades/sharing when conditions are met or impose payment conditions. Sharing includes works to enable sharing.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires conditions for upgrading/sharing apparatus between operators to avoid undue burden on other parties, relevant for cross-border data infrastructure transfers.

sections.source_quote: An operator who has entered into an agreement under Part 2 of this code may, if the conditions in sub-paragraphs (2) and (3) are met—(a) upgrade the electronic communications apparatus to which the agreement relates, or (b) share the use of such electronic communications apparatus with another operator.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Interim and temporary code rights for network maintenance
sections.summary: Courts may impose interim or temporary agreements allowing operators to exercise code rights on land with specific terms. Interim agreements last until a specified event or period ends. Temporary agreements maintain service until proceedings conclude. Both require consideration payments and compensation for losses. Agreements must include terms for payment, duration, and removal of apparatus.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for temporary network access during disputes, relevant for data infrastructure transfers where service continuity is critical.

sections.source_quote: An operator may apply to the court for an order which imposes on the operator and a person, on an interim basis, an agreement between them which—(a) confers a code right on the operator, or (b) provides for a code right to bind that person.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Termination and modification of code agreements with counter-notices
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a code agreement may terminate or be modified following a notice. It specifies that termination occurs after 18 months from notice or when the code right ceases to be exercisable, with exceptions for counter-notices within three months or court applications. The operator and site provider may agree to continue the agreement, and the court may order modifications to terms, termination, or new agreements if they cannot reach agreement.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes procedural safeguards for terminating or modifying code agreements between operators and site providers, including time limits for counter-notices and court intervention. It impacts data transfer DTIAs by defining when agreements can be terminated or modified across borders.

sections.source_quote: The ground stated under sub-paragraph (2)(c) must be one of the following—(a) that the code agreement ought to come to an end as a result of substantial breaches by the operator of its obligations under the agreement; (b) that the code agreement ought to come to an end because of persistent delays by the operator in making payments to the site provider under the agreement; (c) that the site provider intends to redevelop all or part of the land to which the code agreement relates, or any neighbouring land, and could not reasonably do so unless the code agreement comes to an end; (d) that the operator is not entitled to the code agreement because the test under paragraph 21 for the imposition of the agreement on the site provider is not met.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Landowner rights to require removal of electronic communications apparatus
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which landowners can require the removal of electronic communications apparatus from their land. It specifies that landowners must meet five conditions, including that the apparatus is no longer used for the operator's network, the code right has ceased to bind them, and there are no other rights to keep the apparatus. The right is exercisable through notices and court applications if agreements are not reached.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when landowners can require removal of electronic communications apparatus, impacting data transfer DTIAs by establishing conditions for data storage and removal across borders.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Third party enforcement of apparatus removal and court orders
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions under which a third party may apply to court for orders requiring the operator to remove electronic communications apparatus or to sell apparatus. It specifies that if the third party is entitled to enforce removal under paragraph 44(1) or 44(3), they can seek court orders. The court may also require the operator to comply with agreements made under paragraph 44(7) if they fail to do so. The section ensures that court orders do not include provisions from paragraph 44(1)(b) or (3)(d) unless the court deems it appropriate.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Apparatus removal and restoration for land rights
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which a landowner or occupier can require the operator to restore land to its pre-apparatus condition. The operator must comply with restoration notices within a reasonable time, and court orders can enforce restoration or cost recovery. The section ensures that restoration rights are respected without unnecessary apparatus removal.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Street work rights for electronic communications
sections.summary: Operators may install and maintain electronic communications apparatus on streets or roads for statutory purposes. They must not cross land with lines not in place when transport land ceases, nor install new apparatus not in place at that time. Operators can replace existing lines with similar, non-significantly different apparatus. Occupiers may terminate rights after 12 months with notice. Operators face fines for unauthorized works.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border data transfers by requiring physical infrastructure to be in place before installation, with specific timelines for termination and replacement of lines. Relevant for physical data transfer infrastructure.

sections.title: Undertaker's works for electronic communications
sections.summary: Undertakers must notify operators before non-emergency works that interfere with networks. They must specify work nature, apparatus alterations, and start time. Operators can counter-notify to require supervision or make alterations. Undertakers must pay for operator losses and supervision costs. Emergency works require immediate notification and satisfaction of operator.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Rights to object to electronic communications apparatus installation
sections.summary: Operators must install electronic communications apparatus at least three meters above ground. Within three days of installation, operators must attach a notice to major apparatus items with the operator's name and UK address for objections. Objectors can challenge installations within 12 months if the apparatus materially prejudices their land enjoyment or interests, with courts considering costs, service impacts, and compensation. Breaches incur fines up to level 2 on the standard scale.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Tree lopping for electronic communications infrastructure
sections.summary: Operators may require landowners to lop trees or cut vegetation obstructing electronic communications apparatus installed at least three meters above ground. Compensation may be ordered for loss or damage caused by lopping.

It specifies that notices must be sent via registered post or recorded delivery, or delivered to the occupier by description of land. Notices may also be affixed to conspicuous objects on land if delivery is impractical. The section details how notices can be directed to owners through descriptions of land interests and addresses.

If it is not practicable to find the name and address of an occupier, notices may be delivered to the occupier by describing the land
Notices may be delivered to the occupier by affixing to a conspicuous object on land if no person is present
If it is not practicable to find the name and address of an owner, notices may be delivered to the owner by describing the interest and land
Notices may be delivered to the owner by affixing to a conspicuous object on land if no person is present

sections.source_quote: A notice given under this code must not be sent by post unless it is sent by a registered post service or by recorded delivery.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Crown interest definitions and procedures
sections.summary: This section defines 'Crown interest' as interests belonging to Her Majesty in right of the Crown, Duchy of Lancaster, Duchy of Cornwall, government departments, or trusts for Her Majesty. It specifies that agreements and notices related to Crown land must be directed to the appropriate authority, which varies by land type and jurisdiction. The section also outlines procedures for determining the appropriate authority for Crown interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines Crown interests and specifies procedures for handling notices and agreements with Crown land, which may impact data transfers involving government-owned land.

requirements.text: Crown interest includes interests belonging to Her Majesty in right of the Crown, Duchy of Lancaster, Duchy of Cornwall, government departments, or trusts for Her Majesty
Agreements for Crown interest land must be given by the appropriate authority
Notices for Crown interest land must be given by or to the appropriate authority
The appropriate authority varies by land type: Crown Estate Commissioners for Crown land, Chancellor of Duchy of Lancaster for Duchy of Lancaster land, etc.
Any disputes regarding the appropriate authority must be referred to the Treasury

sections.source_quote: Where an agreement is required by this code to be given in respect of any Crown interest subsisting in any land, the agreement must be given by the appropriate authority.

sections.title: Continuation of code rights for subsisting agreements
sections.summary: This section specifies that persons bound by code rights under the existing code continue to be bound by those rights as long as they would be bound if the existing code's paragraph 2(3) remained in effect. It also states that paragraphs 4(4) to (12) of the existing code continue to apply to such persons, with paragraph 4(4)(b) referencing Part 6 of the new code instead of paragraph 21 of the existing code.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures continuity of code rights for existing agreements, which may affect how data transfer mechanisms are maintained during transitions between legal frameworks.

requirements.text: A person bound by a code right by virtue only of paragraph 2(3) of the existing code continues to be bound by it so long as they would be bound if paragraph 2(3) of the existing code continued to have effect.
Paragraphs 4(4) to (12) of the existing code continue to have effect for such persons, with paragraph 4(4)(b) referencing Part 6 of the new code instead of paragraph 21 of the existing code.

sections.source_quote: 181 (a) in relation to the operator and the land to which an agreement for the purposes of paragraph 2 of the existing code relates, references to a right for the statutory purposes to do the things listed in paragraph 2(1)(a) to (c) of the existing code; (b) in relation to land to which an agreement for the purposes of paragraph 3 of the existing code relates, a right to do the things mentioned in that paragraph.

sections.heading: The operator's network (cont.)
sections.title: Continuation of code rights for subsisting agreements
sections.summary: This section outlines that code rights for subsisting agreements continue to apply under the new code, with specific provisions for leases in England and Wales and Northern Ireland. It specifies that certain agreements are exempt from Part 5 of the new code if they meet specific conditions related to the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 or the Business Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses how existing agreements are treated under the new code, which impacts data transfer mechanisms during transitions between legal frameworks.

requirements.text: Part 5 of the new code does not apply to a subsisting agreement that is a lease of land in England and Wales if it is a lease to which Part 2 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 applies and there is no agreement under section 38A of that Act.

Part 5 of the new code does not apply to a subsisting agreement that is a lease of land in England and Wales if the primary purpose of the lease is not to grant code rights and there is an agreement under section 38A of the 1954 Act.
Part 5 of the new code does not apply to a subsisting agreement that is a lease of land in Northern Ireland if it is a lease to which the Business Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 applies.

sections.source_quote: 185 (1) This paragraph applies in relation to a subsisting agreement, in place of paragraph 29(2) to (4) of the new code. (2) Part 5 of the new code does not apply to a subsisting agreement that is a lease of land in England and Wales, if— (a) it is a lease to which Part 2 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 applies, and (b) there is no agreement under section 38A of that Act (agreements to exclude provisions of Part 2) in relation the tenancy.

sections.title: Removal of 'inter-bank' from payment system definitions
sections.summary: This section amends multiple financial regulations to remove the term 'inter-bank' from definitions and references related to payment systems. It applies to the Bank of England, Financial Services Act 2012, and Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. The amendments update the legal definitions to refer to payment systems without the 'inter-bank' qualifier, ensuring consistency across regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that payment systems no longer require the 'inter-bank' designation, which may affect how data transfers are classified under cross-border payment systems. It is relevant for DTIAs involving payment systems but does not introduce new transfer restrictions.
